record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1905 (statewide vital records registration; earlier records at county level, some from 1850)

burned_county_authority: California State Archives, Sacramento; San Francisco is the only county with catastrophic record losses — the 1906 earthquake and fire destroyed most city/county records. Other counties have intact records from formation.
administrative_notes: California was the 31st state, admitted September 9, 1850 under the Compromise of 1850. The territory was ceded by Mexico through the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848). Spanish colonization began with the founding of Mission San Diego de Alcala in 1769. Twenty-one Spanish missions were established along El Camino Real from San Diego to Sonoma (1769-1823). Mexican-era ranchos (land grants) are the foundation of California land records before 1850. The Gold Rush (1848-1855) brought massive immigration from around the world. California has 58 counties — San Francisco is a consolidated city-county since 1856. The 1906 San Francisco earthquake and fire is the defining genealogical catastrophe for the state. Angel Island Immigration Station (1910-1940) processed hundreds of thousands of immigrants, primarily from Asia. California has more federally recognized tribal nations than any other state — over 100 rancherias and reservations.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository. Holdings include state agency records, Spanish and Mexican era documents, county records on microfilm, vital records (births 1905-1910, deaths 1905-present for state copies), prison records, military records, and Supreme Court case files. The Archives also holds naturalization records from various counties and the Great Register voter registration rolls.

Major genealogical research facility. Holdings include San Francisco city directories (1850-present), California county histories, family files, cemetery transcription indexes, newspaper clipping files, California death index (1940-1997), and microfilmed records. The Sutro Branch is the California State Library's genealogy research center.

Premier California history research collection. Holdings include Hubert Howe Bancroft's original manuscript collection, Spanish and Mexican government records, Gold Rush diaries and letters, California pioneer society papers, photographs, and maps. The Bancroft is essential for any serious California genealogical research.

World-class manuscript collection. Holdings include California mission records, Spanish colonial documents, Mexican-era rancho papers, early California family papers, and extensive photographic collections. Essential for Spanish and Mexican era research and mission records.

Federal records for northern California and the Pacific region. Holdings include immigration records (Angel Island), federal court records, military installation records, Bureau of Indian Affairs records, Chinese Exclusion Act case files, and naturalization records. Essential for federal-level California research.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections: county recorder records (deeds, mortgages), vital records, probate records, voter registrations, cemetery records, and church records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records from most California counties. Critical resource for California research.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, Gold Rush research strategies, mission records guide, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes guides to San Francisco 1906 research workarounds.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic California newspapers from the Gold Rush era forward. Includes major titles like the Sacramento Daily Union, San Francisco Chronicle, Los Angeles Times, and hundreds of county newspapers. Essential for obituaries, legal notices, and community events.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: UC Riverside Center for Bibliographical Studies
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized California newspapers from 1846 to the present. Broader coverage than Chronicling America for California, including many small-town newspapers. Free full-text search. Supplements Chronicling America significantly.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Pacific Region (San Bruno / Laguna Niguel)
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for California agencies. California has more federally recognized tribal nations than any other state — over 100 rancherias and reservations. Records include census rolls, allotment records, school records (Sherman Indian School, Stewart Indian School), and correspondence. NARA Record Group 75.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library / Huntington Library
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Mission registers documenting baptisms, marriages, and burials of Native Americans during the Spanish colonial period (1769-1833). The Early California Population Project has digitized many of these records. Essential for tracing Native California families during the mission period.

Sherman Institute (1892-present) student records including enrollment, attendance, health, and correspondence. Students came from tribes across California and the Southwest. One of the longest-operating federal Indian boarding schools. Records at Sherman Indian Museum and NARA.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Pacific Region, San Bruno / Angel Island State Park
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Angel Island Immigration Station (1910-1940) processed hundreds of thousands of immigrants, primarily Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Filipino, South Asian, and other Asian nationalities. Records include investigation case files, interrogation transcripts, photographs, and coaching papers. The 'Ellis Island of the West.' NARA Record Group 85.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files — California
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Case files for Chinese residents and immigrants under the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882-1943). Includes 'paper son' documentation resulting from the 1906 San Francisco fire which destroyed birth records. Files contain photographs, affidavits, family relationship testimony, and community information. Essential for Chinese American genealogy.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Pacific Region / California State Archives
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization proceedings from California state and federal courts. Significant for Gold Rush-era immigrants from worldwide origins. Records include declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificate stubs. County-level naturalizations from 1850 onward.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA / Japanese American National Museum, Los Angeles
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Records of Japanese American internment during WWII. California camps included Manzanar (Inyo County) and Tule Lake (Modoc/Siskiyou counties). Assembly center records for Fresno, Merced, Pomona, Santa Anita, Stockton, Tanforan, Tulare, and Turlock. War Relocation Authority records at NARA Record Group 210.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: California entered the Union as a free state in 1850, but the 1852 California Fugitive Slave Act allowed slaveholders to reclaim enslaved persons brought to California before statehood. Archy Lee case (1858) was a landmark fugitive slave case. Records of African Americans in Gold Rush California, Black newspapers, and community organizations. California State Library and Bancroft Library hold relevant collections.

military_records.description: California National Guard records, WWI draft registration cards, WWII service records, and adjutant general correspondence. Also holds Mexican-American War service records for the California Battalion and records of the Bear Flag Revolt (1846).

military_records.source_name: California Presidio and Military Installation Records (NARA)
military_records.repository: NARA, Washington DC / NARA Pacific Region
military_records.description: Records from California's many military bases: Presidio of San Francisco (1776-1994), Mare Island, Fort Ord, Camp Pendleton, Edwards AFB, and others. Service records, post returns, and garrison records. California has hosted more military installations than any other state.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — California
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for California: homestead entries, preemption claims, mineral patents, timber entries, and military bounty warrants. Searchable online database with document images. Critical for tracing Gold Rush-era claims and homesteading families.

land_and_property.repository: Bancroft Library / NARA / California State Archives
land_and_property.description: Records of approximately 800 Mexican-era land grants (ranchos) issued between 1822 and 1846. US Land Commission confirmed or rejected claims after 1851. Case files include disenos (hand-drawn maps), testimony, chain of title, and surveys. Essential for tracing California families from the Mexican period. Bancroft Library holds the Lands Commission records.

vital_records.source_name: California Department of Public Health — Vital Records
vital_records.repository: CA Dept. of Public Health, Sacramento
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from July 1905 (statewide registration). Marriage and divorce records at county level. For pre-1905 vital events, check county clerk records, church registers, and cemetery records. Historical vital records may be available through California State Archives. San Francisco pre-1906 vital records were largely destroyed.

vital_records.repository: Ancestry.com / California State Library Sutro Branch
vital_records.description: Index of California deaths from 1940-1997 derived from California Department of Health Services data. Includes name, date of death, county, Social Security number, and birth date. Available through Ancestry (subscription) and at Sutro Library. Essential for locating death records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: California Mission Records — Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library
church_and_cemetery.description: Central repository for California Franciscan mission records. Baptismal, marriage, and burial registers from the 21 Alta California missions (1769-1833). The Early California Population Project has indexed many of these records. Essential for Spanish colonial period research.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for California. Coverage varies by county — well-covered urban cemeteries (Hollywood Forever, Mountain View Oakland, Cypress Lawn SF) versus sparse rural and reservation coverage.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society based in Oakland. Library holdings include family files, county histories, and local records. Publishes journal and offers classes on California research methods.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, and research guides for all 58 California counties.

Finding aids and digital collections from California's libraries, archives, and museums. Essential for locating manuscript collections, photographs, and archival records across hundreds of institutions statewide.

historical_counties_extinct.description: California has several extinct and reorganized counties: (1) Klamath County (1851-1874) — abolished, territory divided between Del Norte, Humboldt, and Siskiyou counties. Records scattered among successor counties and California State Archives. (2) Branciforte County was briefly proposed in 1850 but never organized (became Santa Cruz County). (3) The original 27 counties of 1850 have been subdivided into the current 58. (4) San Francisco consolidated with the City of San Francisco in 1856, absorbing the former County of San Francisco boundaries. (5) Several county boundary adjustments occurred throughout the 19th century — researchers must verify which county held jurisdiction for their research location at the relevant time period.

burned_counties_summary.notes: San Francisco is the only California county with catastrophic record losses. The April 18, 1906 earthquake (magnitude 7.9) and subsequent three-day fire destroyed approximately 80% of San Francisco, including City Hall and most government offices. Vital records, court records, and many property records were destroyed. Deed records stored in granite vaults partially survived. The destruction of birth records enabled the 'paper son' phenomenon among Chinese Americans. All other 57 California counties have records intact from their formation dates.
